text: Amdahl's law is a way to estimate how much faster a computer program can run if it's divided into smaller tasks that can be done at the same time on multiple computers. This idea was first suggested by Gene Amdahl in 1967. The law says that a program's speed can only be improved by a certain amount, no matter how many computers are used. This is because some parts of the program can't be split into smaller tasks and must be done one at a time. Even if most of the program can be divided, the overall speed will still be limited by these parts that must be done one at a time. The law can be expressed as a simple equation that shows how the number of computers affects the potential speedup, and it turns out that adding more computers can actually make a program run slower because of these parts that can't be split.

Confucius is often credited with creating a system of thought called Confucianism, but some people think he was more of a teacher who shared existing ideas. His students wrote down his teachings in a book called the Analects, which is still widely read and respected today. Confucius' ideas have spread beyond China to other parts of Asia and have influenced many areas of society, including politics, education, and social norms.

text: The study of plants is called botany. It's a science that looks at how plants grow, develop, and make babies. Botany has many practical uses, such as helping farmers grow food, creating new medicines, and protecting forests.
